seoTitle: Panasonic Technical Support | Application Notes | Authorized Distributor
seoDescription: Technical support for Panasonic capacitors and relays including selection guides, application notes, FAQs, and engineering support. Contact our FAE team for assistance.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Panasonic capacitor for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Panasonic capacitor requires evaluating several key parameters. First, determine the required capacitance value based on your circuit requirements such as ripple current, hold-up time, or filtering needs. Next, select a voltage rating with appropriate derating - typically 20-30% above your maximum operating voltage. Consider the operating temperature range and select a series rated for your application's temperature extremes. For switching power supplies, calculate the ripple current requirements and select a capacitor with adequate ripple current rating and thermal characteristics. Lifetime requirements are critical - Panasonic offers standard, long-life, and ultra-long-life series. Physical constraints such as size, mounting type (radial, SMD, snap-in), and lead spacing must also be considered. Our FAE team can assist with detailed calculations and selection guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team with your application specifications for personalized component recommendations.

faqs.question: What technical documentation is available for Panasonic components?
faqs.answer: Panasonic provides comprehensive technical documentation including datasheets, application notes, reliability data, and qualification reports. Datasheets contain detailed electrical specifications, mechanical dimensions, and performance characteristics. Application notes cover topics such as capacitor selection, ripple current calculations, thermal management, soldering guidelines, and relay coil drive circuits. Reliability data includes lifetime prediction models, failure rate information, and accelerated life test results. For automotive applications, PPAP documentation and AEC-Q200 qualification reports are available. LiTong provides access to all Panasonic technical documentation and can assist with interpretation and application-specific questions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team to request specific technical documentation for your project.

faqs.question: Does LiTong provide FAE support for Panasonic products?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiTong provides comprehensive FAE (Field Application Engineering) support for Panasonic products. Our FAE team has extensive experience with Panasonic capacitors and relays across various applications including power supplies, motor drives, automotive, and consumer electronics. Support services include component selection assistance, thermal analysis, lifetime calculations, schematic review, and troubleshooting. We can provide sample support for evaluation, help interpret datasheets and specifications, and offer design recommendations. For complex applications, our FAEs can perform detailed analysis including ripple current calculations, thermal modeling, and reliability predictions. Contact our FAE team early in your design cycle for optimal results.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to discuss your technical requirements and schedule a design review session.

faqs.question: How can I calculate capacitor lifetime for my application?
faqs.answer: Capacitor lifetime calculation follows the Arrhenius equation, which states that lifetime approximately doubles for every 10C decrease in temperature. The basic formula is: Lx = Lr x 2^((Tr-Tx)/10), where Lx is expected lifetime, Lr is rated lifetime at reference temperature Tr, and Tx is actual operating temperature. Voltage derating also affects lifetime - operating at 80% of rated voltage typically doubles lifetime compared to 100% voltage. Ripple current causes self-heating, which must be added to ambient temperature. Our FAE team can perform detailed lifetime calculations considering all these factors. Panasonic also provides online lifetime calculation tools for quick estimates.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed lifetime calculations specific to your operating conditions.

faqs.question: What is the lead time for Panasonic components?
faqs.answer: Lead times for Panasonic components vary based on product type, specifications, and market demand. Standard aluminum electrolytic capacitors typically have lead times of 8-14 weeks. Film capacitors may require 10-16 weeks depending on specifications. Electromechanical relays in standard configurations usually have 6-10 week lead times. Automotive-qualified components may have longer lead times due to additional testing requirements. LiTong maintains strategic inventory of high-demand Panasonic series to support urgent requirements. For large volume orders, we recommend discussing demand forecasting and scheduling agreements with our sales team to ensure continuous supply.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team with your forecasted volumes and delivery requirements.

faqs.question: Does Panasonic offer automotive-qualified components?
faqs.answer: Yes, Panasonic offers a comprehensive portfolio of automotive-qualified components. Their AEC-Q200 qualified capacitors include aluminum electrolytic and film types specifically designed for automotive applications. These components undergo rigorous qualification testing including temperature cycling, mechanical shock, vibration, and high-temperature operational life testing. Panasonic's automotive relays feature enhanced contact materials and sealing for harsh environments. All automotive-qualified components are manufactured under IATF 16949 quality management systems. Panasonic also provides comprehensive PPAP documentation support for automotive customers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Filter our product listings by automotive qualification, or contact our automotive FAE specialist for component recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I request samples for evaluation?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides sample support for Panasonic component evaluation. To request samples, contact our sales team with the specific part numbers and quantities you need. Please include information about your application, projected annual usage, and target production timeline. This helps us prioritize sample requests and provide appropriate technical support. Standard sample quantities are typically 2-5 pieces per part number. For specialized components, sample availability may be subject to review. We aim to ship standard samples within 3-5 business days.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team with your sample requirements and application information.

faqs.question: What storage and handling requirements apply to Panasonic components?
faqs.answer: Panasonic recommends storing electronic components in controlled conditions. Capacitors should be stored at 5-35C temperature and less than 75% relative humidity. The maximum recommended storage period is 2-3 years from the manufacturing date for electrolytic capacitors. Components should be stored in their original packaging away from direct sunlight, corrosive gases, and dust. Avoid storing near heat sources or in high-humidity environments. Relays should be protected from mechanical shock and vibration during storage. Always follow first-in-first-out inventory practices. For high-reliability applications, consider testing stored components before use.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow recommended storage conditions and use components within recommended timeframes.

articles.title: Aluminum Electrolytic Capacitor Selection Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to selecting aluminum electrolytic capacitors for various applications. Covers capacitance, voltage rating, temperature, ripple current, and lifetime considerations.
content: Aluminum electrolytic capacitors are essential components in power electronics, providing energy storage, filtering, and decoupling functions. Proper selection is critical for reliable operation and long service life.

The required capacitance depends on the application. For power supply output filtering, calculate based on allowable ripple voltage and load current. For DC bus applications, consider both ripple requirements and energy storage needs.

Always apply voltage derating for reliability. A general rule is to operate at 70-80% of the rated voltage. This significantly extends capacitor lifetime - operating at 80% of rated voltage can double the expected life compared to 100% voltage.

Select capacitors with temperature ratings appropriate for your application environment. Standard ratings are 85C and 105C. Higher temperature ratings provide longer life at the same operating temperature due to the Arrhenius relationship.

Ripple current causes self-heating in capacitors. Calculate the expected ripple current in your application and select capacitors with adequate ripple current rating. Always include margin - at least 20% above calculated values.

Consider the expected service life of your product. Standard capacitors offer 2000-5000 hours at rated temperature, while long-life series provide 10000-12000 hours. Calculate expected lifetime at your actual operating conditions.
For output filtering, prioritize low ESR for ripple reduction. For DC bus applications, ensure adequate ripple current capability and consider parallel configurations for high-current designs.
DC bus capacitors must handle high ripple current from inverter switching. Select capacitors with appropriate ripple current rating and consider voltage transients from regenerative braking.
Use AEC-Q200 qualified capacitors for automotive applications. Ensure adequate temperature margin for under-hood environments. Consider vibration requirements.
- Selecting based only on capacitance value
Contact our FAE team for assistance with your specific application requirements.

faeInsights.insight: In my 15 years of supporting capacitor applications, I've seen many designs fail due to improper capacitor selection. The most common mistake is focusing only on capacitance value while ignoring ripple current requirements. I always tell engineers to calculate the actual operating conditions first - temperature, voltage, and ripple current - then select capacitors with appropriate margins.
faeInsights.logic: The capacitor selection process should follow a systematic approach: define application requirements, calculate operating conditions, identify candidate series, verify all specifications, and apply appropriate derating.
keyTakeaways: Always calculate actual operating conditions before selecting capacitors
Apply voltage derating of at least 20% for reliability
Include 20% margin on ripple current ratings
commonPitfalls: Selecting based only on capacitance value
bestPractices: Use manufacturer lifetime models for prediction

customerCases.challenge: The customer experienced capacitor failures in their power supply after 2 years of field operation due to inadequate ripple current capability.
customerCases.solution: We recommended upgrading to Panasonic FR series capacitors with 50% higher ripple current rating and improved thermal design.
customerCases.results: The redesigned power supply achieved calculated lifetime of 15+ years with zero failures reported.

faqs.question: What is the most important parameter when selecting capacitors?
faqs.answer: While capacitance value is important, ripple current capability and temperature rating are often more critical for reliability. Ripple current causes self-heating, which dramatically affects lifetime.
faqs.decisionGuide: Prioritize ripple current and temperature ratings alongside capacitance for reliable selection.

articles.title: Electromechanical Relay Selection Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to selecting electromechanical relays for various applications. Covers contact configuration, load types, coil voltage, and arc suppression considerations.
content: Electromechanical relays are essential components for switching electrical loads in control systems. Proper selection ensures reliable operation, long contact life, and safe switching of various load types.

Select the appropriate contact configuration based on your circuit requirements: SPST for simple on/off switching, SPDT for selecting between two circuits, DPDT for simultaneous switching of two circuits.

Identify your load type: resistive, inductive, or capacitive. Each type presents different challenges. Inductive loads like motors create high inrush currents and inductive kickback. Size relays for inrush current, not just running current.
AgSnO2 is recommended for inductive loads due to anti-welding properties. Au-clad is preferred for low-level signal switching. Standard silver is suitable for resistive loads.
Match the relay coil voltage to your control system. Common options are 5V, 12V, 24V, and 48V DC. Ensure your driving circuit can supply the required coil current.
Consider your switching frequency and required operational life. Electrical life is typically much shorter than mechanical life due to contact arcing under load.

Inductive loads require arc suppression to protect relay contacts. RC snubbers using film capacitors are effective for AC and DC applications. Varistors and TVS diodes provide alternative protection methods.
- Using standard contacts for motor loads
Contact our FAE team for assistance with relay selection.

faeInsights.insight: The most common relay failures I see are from contact welding on motor loads. Engineers often select relays based on running current without considering the 5-10x inrush current. AgSnO2 contacts are essential for motor applications - they cost a bit more but prevent the contact welding that ruins standard relays.
faeInsights.logic: Relay selection must consider both the steady-state load and transient conditions. Motor loads are particularly challenging due to high inrush current and inductive kickback.
keyTakeaways: Use AgSnO2 contacts for inductive loads
Size relays for inrush current, not just running current
Implement arc suppression for inductive loads
Consider electrical life, not just mechanical life
commonPitfalls: Using standard silver contacts for motors
bestPractices: Always use AgSnO2 for motor and solenoid loads
Include RC snubbers for inductive switching
Consider solid state relays for frequent switching

customerCases.challenge: Relay contact welding issues in fan motor control systems due to motor inrush currents.
customerCases.solution: Upgraded to Panasonic ALDP relays with AgSnO2 contacts and added RC snubber circuits.
customerCases.results: Contact welding eliminated, relay life increased 4x, service calls reduced by 80%.
customerCases.quantifiedResults: 4x relay life increase, 80% reduction in service calls

faqs.question: Why do relays fail when switching motor loads?
faqs.answer: Motor inrush current (5-10x running current) can weld contacts together. Inductive kickback creates arcing that erodes contacts. Solutions include AgSnO2 contacts and snubber circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use AgSnO2 contact relays and implement snubber circuits for reliable motor switching.
